context:
This course examines how a variety of cultural traditions propose answers to the question of how to live a meaningful life. It considers the meaning of life, not as a philosophical abstraction, but as a question that individuals grapple with in their daily lives, facing difficult decisions between meeting and defying cultural expectations. The course also provides tools for thinking about moral decisions as social and historical practices, and permits students to compare and contextualize the ways people in different times and places approach fundamental ethical concerns.

Syllabus:
21A.157 The Meaning of Life

Course Meeting Times:
- Lectures: 2 sessions/week; 1.5 hours/session

Prerequisites:
There are no prerequisites for this course.

Course Description:
This course examines how a variety of cultural traditions approach and propose answers to the question of how to live a meaningful life. We consider the meaning of life, not as a philosophical abstraction, but as a matter with which individuals grapple in their daily lives and communities, facing difficult decisions between meeting and defying cultural expectations. The course aims to provide tools for thinking about moral and ethical decisions as social and historical practices, and permits students to compare and contextualize how people in different times and places approach fundamental — though not necessarily universal — questions to do with such topics as family, wealth, self-cultivation, sex, belonging, and powers beyond.

Text:

Required:
Hamdy, Sherine, and Coleman Nye. Lissa: A Story about Medical Promise, Friendship, and Revolution. University of Toronto Press, 2017. ISBN: 9781487593476. [Preview with Google Books]

Suggested:
Kavedžija, Iza, and Harry Walker, eds. Values of Happiness: Toward an Anthropology of Purpose in Life. HAU, 2017. ISBN: 9780986132575.

All other readings can be found in the Readings section.

Grading:

| Activities                            | Percentage |
|---------------------------------------|------------|
| Attendance and participation          | 15%        |
| "Moral Moment" vignette               | 5%         |
| Investigation papers (3 × 20% each)   | 60%        |
| Final paper                           | 20%        |

Course context:
This course examines how a variety of cultural traditions propose answers to the question of how to live a meaningful life. It considers the meaning of life, not as a philosophical abstraction, but as a question that individuals grapple with in their daily lives, facing difficult decisions between meeting and defying cultural expectations. The course also provides tools for thinking about moral decisions as social and historical practices, and permits students to compare and contextualize the ways people in different times and places approach fundamental ethical concerns.

Syllabus:
21A.157 The Meaning of Life

Course Meeting Times:
- Lectures: 2 sessions/week; 1.5 hours/session

Prerequisites:
There are no prerequisites for this course.

Course Description:
This course examines how a variety of cultural traditions approach and propose answers to the question of how to live a meaningful life. We consider the meaning of life, not as a philosophical abstraction, but as a matter with which individuals grapple in their daily lives and communities, facing difficult decisions between meeting and defying cultural expectations. The course aims to provide tools for thinking about moral and ethical decisions as social and historical practices, and permits students to compare and contextualize how people in different times and places approach fundamental — though not necessarily universal — questions to do with such topics as family, wealth, self-cultivation, sex, belonging, and powers beyond.

Text:

Required:
Hamdy, Sherine, and Coleman Nye. Lissa: A Story about Medical Promise, Friendship, and Revolution. University of Toronto Press, 2017. ISBN: 9781487593476. [Preview with Google Books]

Suggested:
Kavedžija, Iza, and Harry Walker, eds. Values of Happiness: Toward an Anthropology of Purpose in Life. HAU, 2017. ISBN: 9780986132575.

Grading:

| Activities                            | Percentage |
|---------------------------------------|------------|
| Attendance and participation          | 15%        |
| "Moral Moment" vignette               | 5%         |
| Investigation papers (3 × 20% each)   | 60%        |
| Final paper                           | 20%        |

Exercise:
TITLE: Investigation Papers

21A.157 The Meaning of Life
Massachusetts Institute of Technology

Weight: 60% of the final grade (3 papers × 20% each)

Overview:
Students will complete three (3) Investigation Papers (1500–1600 words each). Five of the subject modules we cover offer two options for Investigation Papers. Pick three modules on which to write, and for each select one Investigation option. Plan in advance which Investigations you intend to do, since some involve coordinating with other people or travel to off-campus destinations. Each assignment requires collecting anthropological data using methods such as participant observation or interviewing, and then analyzing your findings through the lens of related readings. You must cite, quote, and engage relevant readings from the class in your papers.


Module 1: Family
Due: Session 6

Option 1:
Interview someone who is or has been married about their expectations and experiences of the widespread yet culturally varied social institution of marriage. In what ways do their experiences match or diverge from their expectations before being married? To what extent have they experienced marriage as a "moral laboratory" (refer to Cheryl Mattingly's article "Moral Selves and Moral Scenes: Narrative Experiments in Everyday Life")? In their view, how has marriage or "being married" changed over time, from (say) their parents' generation to their own? What have been the gains and losses of these changes, for whom? If the interviewee is an immigrant or the child of immigrants, how does cultural difference factor into their experience and assessment? Be sure to engage and cite at least two of our readings in your discussion.

Option 2:
Write an Op-Ed in (at least partial) response to Amy Chua's "Why Chinese Mothers are Superior." You may argue against her piece or write in praise of it — either way, your piece should speak from the perspective of a parent's child. You do not need to speak directly from your own personal experience (although you may) but rather from that of your generation more broadly — think of your friends' experiences, of discussions you've seen unfold over social media, perhaps. Be sure to engage the insights of and cite at least two other readings (Hirsch, Kuan, Mattingly, Kolbert) in your discussion.


Module 2: Wealth
Due: Session 10

Option 1:
Interview someone about what money means to them. How is it connected with independence or interdependence? How do they think about earning money? About sharing, lending, or giving money? Do they receive money from their family, send money to their family, or some combination? Does money imply freedom or servitude? What is money good for? Has their relationship to money changed over time? Is money a source of pride or shame? What does debt mean to them? Do they view money differently from other people in their family (particularly people of other generations) or their peers? Do they feel like their views about money are in line with the rest of society? Be sure to engage and cite at least two of our readings in your discussion.

Option 2:
Interview someone about their job search or employment history. This could be someone who is currently employed, unemployed, or even retired. In addition to documenting past and present jobs and future career aspirations, explore what work means to this person. Do they live to work or work to live? To what extent does their sense of identity or personal worth come from work? Do they fantasize about changing jobs? How much did they train for their current position? Are they satisfied with their current situation (in terms of responsibilities, opportunities, relationships with co-workers, remuneration, etc.)? Why or why not? Is their work adequately appreciated by others? Why or why not? Is the job getting better or worse? Be sure to engage and cite at least two of our readings in your discussion.


Module 3: Sex
Due: Session 14

Option 1:
Find and analyze a piece (or pieces) of dating and/or sex advice: this could be from the past or the present; something you deem as helpful or harmful. Who's the presumed audience for this advice (and who's left out)? What is presumed about what 'sex' is and why it's important — and to whom? What broader values or virtues — e.g., about human nature, about pleasure, about gender (being "good" at being a man or woman), about morality (being a "good" person) — are encoded, implicitly or explicitly, in the advice given? What do you find helpful and/or harmful about this advice, to whom, and why? Be sure to engage and cite at least two of our readings in your discussion.

Option 2:
Write a dating and/or sex advice column for a specific audience of your choosing (be sure to specify). Keeping your target audience in mind, what do you think they ought better to understand about what sex is (and is not), what it means (culturally, morally, legally, etc.), and what its rightful place should be in people's (again, be specific) lives. Think about the best way to convey this message to your audience (through anecdotal illustration, in terms of tone, etc.). Explain your reasoning as you go. Be sure to engage and cite at least two of our readings in your discussion, either as part of your main text, or in footnotes, or as a coda.


Module 4: Self-Cultivation
Due: Session 19

Option 1:
Conduct a week-long self-tracking exercise with the aid of an app (e.g. time management, dietary intake, exercise, sleep or mood tracking). Describe what you did and why you chose the activity/app you did. Analyze not only the data that resulted, but also the experience of tracking. What surprised you about the exercise, and/or results? Did awareness of tracking affect your behavior or thoughts at the time, regardless of the data? What benefits, or losses, did you experience, or do you think would accrue over time if you were to continue the practice? Will you try to continue? Why or why not? In analyzing your findings, draw on at least two readings from this module (and not only the media pieces assigned for recitation).

Option 2:
Interview a friend or relative about a hobby or avocation. If possible, accompany them to engage in the activity and, ideally, try to do it with them. How important is the hobby to their sense of self? Has involvement with the activity changed them over time and, if so, how? What kinds of qualities are necessary to excel at the activity, and do those qualities translate into other aspects of life? Is there a community associated with this practice and, if so, how important is membership in that community to your interviewee? Does the hobby help to relieve stress or does it have stresses of its own? In the context of this activity, are there accomplishments that the person is particularly proud of? How important is challenging oneself to keeping the activity fun or gratifying? In analyzing your findings, draw on at least two readings from this module.

Student Example:
"Self-Cultivation Investigation Paper - Option 2" (PDF)


Module 5: Belonging
Due: Session 23

Option 1:
Visit a cultural institution or historical site associated with collective cultural memory that raises questions concerning belonging (e.g., Boston's Museum of African American History and/or Black Heritage Trail, the Jewish Heritage Museum, the West End immigration museum — you might also look to see if there are current art exhibits or other relevant cultural events to attend). What is the central narrative about belonging (and/or exclusion) presented at the site or exhibit? Whom does the site seem to address as its audience(s)? If interpretative materials are available, what do they say about the meaning and significance of the site or event commemorated, and to whom? How does the site, or the history to which it speaks, connect to the social and physical environment around it today? You may wish to conduct this site visit with a friend who has a meaningful personal connection with the place or history evoked; if so, how does this affect your own experience? Be sure to engage and cite at least two of our readings in your discussion.

Option 2:
Identify an object that represents a sense of belonging — to a particular place, an ethnic group, a religion, a nation, etc. (an entity broader than 'family') — for yourself or someone you know. (If the object is yours, offer your own reflections in this assignment; if the object is someone else's, interview the owner to address the following questions.) What is the history of the object, and how did its owner come to acquire it? What stories does it contain? To what larger group or history or community does it connect the owner? Where does the object reside in its owner's room/home (e.g., is it hidden away or put openly on display)? What can we learn from this object and its personal significance to its owner about the cultural significance and politics of belonging more broadly? Be sure to engage and cite at least two of our readings in your discussion.

Student Example:
"Belonging Investigation Paper - Option 2" (PDF)

Note: Student examples appear courtesy of MIT students and are anonymous by request.

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: Uh, I’m kind of lost on the Investigation Paper. I get that I have to do an interview or observation thing, but I honestly don’t really understand what the paper is supposed to look like or how I’m supposed to connect my data to the readings.
exchanges.tutor: That's a fair starting point — let's work through it together. Before we get into structure, which module and option are you thinking of writing about? That'll help us focus the conversation on something concrete.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is expressing general confusion about the assignment. Rather than explaining the structure for them, I should use a Socratic question to get them to engage with what they already understand, and help them build from there. I'll acknowledge their concern and ask a focusing question to get them started on the right track without over-scaffolding.

exchanges.student: That’s part of why I’m stuck — I keep changing my mind. Family, Wealth, or Self-Cultivation all seem possible, and they all kind of blur together for me, so I don’t really know how different the papers are supposed to be. Could you maybe just explain what these papers generally want, because I feel like I’m missing the big picture first?
exchanges.tutor: Fair enough — but before I explain anything, re-read the Overview section of the assignment and tell me in your own words: what do you think the two main tasks of these papers are? Take a guess, even if you're not sure.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking for a general explanation of the assignment before committing to a topic. This is a reasonable request, but I should be careful not to over-explain or do the interpretive work for them. I'll ask them to read the assignment overview again and reflect on what they think the core task is — connecting empirical data to course readings. I want to prompt them to articulate the big picture themselves rather than hand it to them.

exchanges.student: I think one part is doing some kind of fieldwork thing, like interviewing someone or tracking something, and the other part is… talking about it with course readings? But I don’t really know what “analyzing” means here versus just summarizing what happened, and that’s the part that makes the whole assignment feel fuzzy to me.
exchanges.tutor: Good — you've got the two parts right. Now think about this: if you interviewed someone about money and they said 'I feel guilty spending on myself,' what's the difference between just reporting that versus analyzing it? What would analyzing that moment actually require you to do?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has correctly identified the two main components. Now I should affirm that and push them to think about the distinction between summarizing and analyzing, which is the crux of their confusion. I'll use a Socratic question to get them to reason through this themselves rather than explaining it directly.

exchanges.student: I guess reporting is just saying they felt guilty, and analyzing is saying why? But then I get confused because I don’t know if “why” means their personal reasons, or like a bigger cultural reason, or what the reading says it means. I’m worried I’ll either make it too personal or too abstract.
exchanges.tutor: You're actually onto something important there. So — what do you think the readings are *for* in this assignment? Why do you think the instructions specifically ask you to 'analyze through the lens of the readings'?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is actually reasoning well and getting close to understanding analysis. They've identified the tension between personal/individual explanation and broader cultural/theoretical explanation. This is a productive insight. I should affirm it and push them to think about how the readings might serve as a bridge between those two levels, rather than explaining it myself.

exchanges.student: Maybe the readings are supposed to give me concepts or language for what I noticed? I’m just still not seeing how that turns into paragraphs, like do I organize by interview topics, by themes, by each reading, or something else? I feel like I need a super basic picture of what a decent paper even looks like.
exchanges.tutor: That instinct about the readings — giving you concepts and language — is exactly right, hold onto that. But here's the thing: structural questions are much easier to answer once you're working with real material. You've been circling the big picture for a bit now. Pick one module and option, even tentatively, and let's see what the paper actually starts to look like in practice.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is making good progress conceptually but is now pivoting to asking about structure/format, which risks becoming a substitute for actually engaging with the assignment. I should affirm their insight about readings providing conceptual language — that's genuinely correct — but resist giving them a template. Instead, I should redirect them toward picking a topic so we can work concretely, since abstract structural questions are harder to resolve without a specific case. The student has been circling general questions for several exchanges; it's time to push toward commitment.

exchanges.student: Okay, maybe Wealth option 1, interviewing someone about money. But even with that, I still feel blank because “money” is huge and I can already imagine the interview going in ten directions, so I don’t know what would count as enough material versus too much.
exchanges.tutor: Good choice, let's work with that. Go back and re-read the Wealth Option 1 prompt carefully. What specific themes or questions does it already suggest you explore? List a few — that should help with the 'ten directions' feeling.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: Good, the student has committed to a topic. Now they're worried about scope/focus during the interview. Rather than telling them how to focus it, I should ask them to look at the assignment prompt itself and identify what it's actually asking — there are concrete guiding questions already in the prompt that can help them focus. This will also help them realize they don't need to invent the structure from scratch.

exchanges.student: It mentions independence vs interdependence, earning, sharing or giving money, family, debt, pride or shame, freedom or servitude, changes over time, and differences across generations. That’s kind of exactly why I’m overwhelmed though, because it feels like all of those could each become their own paper and I don’t know how people are supposed to narrow it without missing the assignment.
exchanges.tutor: Those are guides for the interview, not a checklist you have to cover completely. Think about it this way: after you do the interview, what do you think will determine which of those themes you actually write about?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has done a good job listing the themes from the prompt. Now they're overwhelmed by the breadth. I should help them realize that these are interview prompts/guides, not required sections to cover exhaustively. A Socratic nudge toward thinking about what emerges organically from an actual interview — and what the readings might help illuminate — will help them see that focus comes from the data and the readings, not from trying to cover everything.

exchanges.student: I mean probably whichever themes actually come up strongly? But then that makes me nervous too, because what if the interview is kind of messy or the person talks about random stuff and I can’t tell what the “real” theme is. I kind of want a general example of how a messy interview becomes an actual argument.
exchanges.tutor: Messiness is actually normal in fieldwork — that's not a problem to solve before the interview. Here's a useful move: the assignment page includes student example papers for other modules. Have you looked at those? They might show you more concretely than I can how someone turned real material into an argument.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking for an example of how a messy interview becomes an argument — this is essentially asking me to model the analytical process for them, which risks doing their thinking for them. I should redirect this toward the readings, since the course actually provides student examples for other modules (Self-Cultivation and Belonging). I can point them there without giving them a template. I should also gently note that messiness in interviews is normal and that the readings are precisely the tool for finding meaning in that mess.

exchanges.student: Not really. I think I avoided them because I was hoping someone would just tell me the formula, and now I’m still kind of hoping for that. Could you just give me the most basic possible template for turning interview stuff plus 2 readings into a paper?
exchanges.tutor: I'm not going to give you a template — that's not my role, and honestly a formula would probably hurt more than help since the paper needs to come from your specific interview and your own analytical choices. But you have a real shortcut available: those student examples exist precisely for this moment. Read one, and then come back and tell me what you notice about how it's put together. That'll be more useful than any template I could give you.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is directly asking for a template, which I should refuse as it would bypass their own thinking and learning. I should be direct and honest that I won't do this, explain briefly why, and redirect them toward the student examples which are literally provided for this purpose. This is a good moment to be clear about my role.

exchanges.student: Okay, but I’m worried I won’t even know what I’m supposed to be noticing in the example. I’ll probably just read it and think “yeah this sounds smart” without seeing how it’s built. Could you at least tell me what 2 or 3 things I should look for when I read it?
exchanges.tutor: You already told me earlier what confuses you most: how interview material connects to readings. So read the example with just that one question in mind — where and how does the author bring in a reading, and what is it doing at that moment? Come back and tell me what you find.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to pre-digest the example for them by giving them a reading guide. This is still avoiding doing their own thinking. However, they've expressed a genuine concern about not knowing what to look for, which is somewhat reasonable. I can ask them one focused question that will orient their reading without giving them a checklist — pointing them toward the connection between data and readings, which is the core of what they've said they find confusing. This keeps the scaffolding minimal.
